coredata.dc:title: Tunable (violet to green) emission by high-yield graphene quantum dots and exploiting its unique properties towards sun-light-driven photocatalysis and supercapacitor electrode materials
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Sumeet
Ojha, Animesh K.
Ahmed, Bilal
Kumar, Ashok
Das, Jayanta
Materny, Arnulf
coredata.dc:description: The precise control of dimension and mono-dispersibility of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is still a challenge and new developments in this field are of great interest for scientific community. In this work, we have investigated the feasibility of the production of mono-dispersed spherical GQDs (∼4nm) based on a treatment solely with ammonia solution as both, reducing and stabilizing agent. The structure, size and shape of the so synthesized GQDs were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Raman spectroscopy measurements. The synthesized GQDs show excitation-dependent tunable color emission (violet to green). These GQDs could be potentially used as biomarkers for florescent bioimaging. The GQDs also prove to be excellent sunlight-driven photocatalysts for the photodegradation (45%) of methylene blue (MB) dye. Therefore, these GQDs could find remarkable application in the field of photocatalysis. Electrochemical measurements indicate good rate capability and stability of the GQDs in view of their use as supercapacitor electrode material. The present work not only provides a better explanation of the experimental observations, but also suggests an efficient method for the controllable synthesis of multi-functional GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrothermal
GQDs
Tunable emission
Photocatalytic activity
Supercapacitor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram for the synthesis of GQDs.
XRD patterns for GQDs (a) and graphite (b).
TEM images at different resolutions (a–b), EDX spectrum (c) and histogram (d) of GQDs.
UV–vis spectrum of GQDs. Inset: Photograph of the aqueous dispersion of GQDs.
PL emission spectra of GQDs excited with light of different wavelengths (a) and relationship between the energy of emission light and excitation light (b).
Schematic illustration of typical electronic transition process of GQDs. Diagram of Stokes transition (a) and the electronic transitions between energy levels of GQDs (b).

Absorption spectra of aqueous solution of MB dye exposed under direct sunlight at different exposure times (a) photodegradation rate of pure MB dye solution and MB dye solution mixed with GQDs as photocatalysts under direct sunlight irradiation (b) and recycling performance of GQDs for the degradation of MB (c).
Mechanism for photocatalytic degradation of MB dye with GQDs.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) profiles of GQDs. CV profiles of GQDs at different scan rates (a), CV profiles of GQDs at scan rate 10mV/s for first five cycles (b), specific capacitance vs. scan rate (c), and energy density vs. scan rate (d).

head.ce:title: Tunable (violet to green) emission by high-yield graphene quantum dots and exploiting its unique properties towards sun-light-driven photocatalysis and supercapacitor electrode materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Tezpur University, Assam 784028, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Motilal Nehru National Institute of Technology Allahabad, 211004, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Physics, Motilal Nehru National Institute of Technology, Allahabad 211004, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Motilal Nehru National Institute of Technology Allahabad

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The precise control of dimension and mono-dispersibility of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is still a challenge and new developments in this field are of great interest for scientific community. In this work, we have investigated the feasibility of the production of mono-dispersed spherical GQDs (∼4nm) based on a treatment solely with ammonia solution as both, reducing and stabilizing agent. The structure, size and shape of the so synthesized GQDs were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Raman spectroscopy measurements. The synthesized GQDs show excitation-dependent tunable color emission (violet to green). These GQDs could be potentially used as biomarkers for florescent bioimaging. The GQDs also prove to be excellent sunlight-driven photocatalysts for the photodegradation (45%) of methylene blue (MB) dye. Therefore, these GQDs could find remarkable application in the field of photocatalysis. Electrochemical measurements indicate good rate capability and stability of the GQDs in view of their use as supercapacitor electrode material. The present work not only provides a better explanation of the experimental observations, but also suggests an efficient method for the controllable synthesis of multi-functional GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: The discovery of graphene has attracted greatest attention by the scientific and technological community because of its remarkable structural, optical, and electronic properties. Its linear band structure makes this substance a highly interesting candidate for potential applications in single electron transistors and flexible electronics [1–6]. However, the zero optical bandgap of graphene limits its optoelectronic applications [7]. The lack of optical bandgap in graphene obstructs the possibility of observing luminescence, which in turn prevents the improvement of new technologies based on this property [8]. In this context, creating an optical bandgap as source of photoluminescence (PL) emission in graphene is an important objective among the worldwide researchers. An optical bandgap could be opened in graphene by confining its physical dimensions in the range of nanometer scale. The reduction in size down to 10nm induces a high degree of quantum confinement and edge effects, which may induce an optical bandgap in graphene due to the modification of the band structure [9,10]. A number of studies [5–11] were done on inducing the bandgap in graphene to exhibit strong PL by controlling its size and shape. Ritter et al. [5] and Lu et al. [11] have clearly demonstrated the size dependence of the optical band gap of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Peng et al. [7] have also reported the blue shift of PL emission of GQDs with decreasing size due to increased value of optical band gap.

Thus, GQDs have been validated as a new kind of luminescent carbon nanomaterials [10]. GQDs are superior compared to semiconductor quantum dots because of their excellent photostability, chemical inertness, reduced toxicity, high biocompatibility, and unique optoelectronic properties [12,13]. GQDs clearly possess the structure of graphene internally with outstanding physical and chemical properties, such as large surface area, bigger length to diameter ratio, i.e. the ratio of length to thickness, and superior surface grafting through the π–π conjugated network [14]. The primary challenge for the prospective applications of GQDs in next generation electronic and optoelectronic devices is their efficient and scalable production from graphene oxide (GO) with simultaneous reduction of GO. Many efforts have been made for the reduction of GO using different methods and reducing agents [15–22]. Among them, chemical reduction of GO has been considered as an efficient and cost effective method. Since in chemical reduction of GO, the quality and reduction level is determined by reducing agents used during the synthesis, different kinds of reducing agents such as hydrazine [23], sulphuric acid [24], sodiumborohydride [25], L-ascorbic acid [26] and ethylenediamine [27] have been used by researchers. However, these reducing agents have their own merits and demerits. Although, on the one hand, they show as obvious advantage a remarkable reduction level, on the other hand, they suffer from the use of additional stabilizing agents for stability and controlling the size and shape of graphene. To avoid the need of an extra stabilizing agent, some researchers have applied materials, which act as reducing as well as stabilizing agent simultaneously. Zhang et al. [26] have demonstrated that l-ascorbic acid (L-AA) can be used as reducing agent and the oxidized products of L-AA may play an important role to stabilize GO after reduction. Recently, Liu et al. [28] have reported that the use of benzylamine serves the purpose of both, reducing and stabilizing agent for graphene. In another work, chitosan has also been used as a reducing as well as stabilizing agent for the formation of reduced graphene oxide (RGO) [29]. However, these reducing and stabilizing agents have not been used so far for synthesizing GQDs. Further, the problem related with the aggregation of GQDs in aqueous medium is also a great challenge for the researchers due to the existence of strong van-der-Waals interactions occurring there. The aggregation of GODs may be addressed nicely using stabilizing agents and controlling the pH value during the synthesis [28].

Thus, in view of the above reported studies on graphene and graphene quantum dots and their applications in various field of science and technology, it is still a big challenge for scientists to find suited reducing agents that serve the purpose of both, reducing and stabilizing agents simultaneously for the synthesis of GQDs with excellent crystallinity, monodispersity, and controllable size and shape.

Another interesting field of application for the GQDs can be found in the challenging field of photocatalysis. The direct conversion of solar energy into chemical energy is an important goal due to environmental and energetic advantages [30]. Electrochemical activity is an important issue for applications in this connection [31]. Recently, graphene derivatives have been found to be promising photocatalytic and electrode materials due to their abundance, low cost, large surface area, high conductivity, and wide electrochemical window [32,33]. It should be noted that graphene derivatives have developed mainly as supports of semiconductors to enhance their photocatalytic and electrochemical efficiency. The use of graphene derivatives as standalone photocatalytic and electrode materials has only been rarely considered. However, the study of photocatalytic and electrochemical properties of individual graphene derivatives could be of great interest. Due to their fascinating and tunable properties, GQDs have now attracted great attention in their potential application related to environment and energy issues. Some works on the use of GQDs as photocatalytic and electrode materials have been reported in literature [9,34]. However, to the best of our knowledge, no work on GQDs addresses both the photocatalytic and electrochemical activities simultaneously.

In the present contribution, we demonstrate a simple and cost-effective hydrothermal method for the formation of monodispersed, spherical GQDs based on the use of ammonia (NH3) solution acting as both, reducing and stabilizing agent. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on the synthesis of well-dispersed, spherical and relatively small (4–5nm) GQDs using NH3 as reducing and stabilizing agent. The hydrothermal method described in the present study, provides a convenient pathway allowing for a well-controlled synthesis of GQDs with an excellent reduction level analogous to that reached by previously reported NH3 solution-based sodium metal and formamidinesulfinic acid mixtures [35–37]. The possible mechanisms for the growth process of monodispersed and spherical GQDs will be discussed. The synthesized products have been characterized by different techniques such as X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Raman spectroscopy (RS), ultra-violet visible (UV–vis) absorption spectroscopy, and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. Here, we have focused on the study of the excitation-dependent PL emission properties of synthesized GQDs, and possible mechanisms for a tunable emission have been described in-depth in order to determine their possible optoelectronic applications. In addition to this, we also discuss some fundamental aspects of the unique properties of GQDs related to photocatalysis and electrochemical activity. Here, an objective of this work is to provide some insights related to the use of pure graphene derivatives, particularly GQDs, in photocatalytic as well as electrochemical applications. The electrochemical performance of GQDs and direct sunlight driven photodegradation of methylene blue (MB) dye using GQDs are discussed in this report.

At first, graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized by an approach reported by us in detail recently [32]. Subsequently, the GO powder was used to produce GQDs by a facile one step in-situ hydrothermal method. In a typical experiment, as synthesized brown solid powder of GO (40mg) was first dispersed in 40ml double-distilled water under mild stirring. The dispersed GO was then exfoliated by ultrasonication at room temperature for half an hour. Afterwards, 200μl of NH3 solution were added at once into the above mentioned solution under mild stirring at room temperature and subsequently, the solution was transferred to a 50ml teflon-lined autoclave, heated to 140°C and kept at this temperature for 12h. The autoclave was then cooled to room temperature. The obtained precipitate was washed several times with double-distilled water and ethanol followed by centrifugation at 4000rpm. The remaining solid material was then dried at 60°C and the resulting black powder of GQDs was collected for further use. A schematic of the synthesis steps resulting in GQDs is presented in Fig. 1 .

XRD measurements were performed using a Rigaku Smart Lab X-ray diffractometer with CuKα radiation at a wavelength of λ=1.540Å to determine the crystalline phase and lattice constant of the synthesized samples. The shape and size were investigated using (HR-TEM) measurements performed on a JEOL JEM-2100 TEM at 200kV (JEOL Co. Ltd.). Room-temperature Raman spectra of the powder samples were recorded in the spectral range of 1200–2800cm−1 using a T64000 triple Raman micro-spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon). The 514.5nm laser line of an Ar+ ion laser was used for excitation. The laser power on the sample was fixed to 1mW. The spectra were recorded for 10s exposure time under a microscope using a 50X objective. For recording the spectra, the slit width was kept at 100μm. A PerkinElmer (Lambda35) UV–vis absorption spectrometer in absorbance mode was used for measuring the absorption spectra of the prepared samples. PL measurements were done using a PerkinElmer (LS-55) fluorescence spectrometer. The samples were excited by various excitation wavelengths and emission spectra were recorded in the spectral range of 375–600nm.

Sunlight-driven photocatalytic measurements of synthesized GQDs were carried out to demonstrate the efficiency in the degradation reaction of MB dye. The photodegradation catalyzed by the GQDs was investigated by observing the decolorization of an aqueous solution of 10−5 M MB dye. The rate of the bleaching was recorded by measuring the intensity of the characteristic peak in the absorption spectrum of MB. For the experiment, 50mg GQDs as photocatalyst were added to 50ml of aqueous solution of MB dye and the suspension was subjected to irradiation under sunlight. The aqueous suspension was continuously stirred throughout the experiment. At regular time intervals, the sample was taken out with the help of a syringe and filtered. Then absorption spectra of the irradiated solution were recorded and the bleaching rate recorded in terms of the change in intensity at the maximum absorption (λmax =664nm) of MB dye. The decolorization efficiency (%) has been calculated as: efficiency (%)=
C 0
− C
C
· 100 %
where C 0 is the initial concentration of MB dye and C is the concentration of MB dye after irradiation.

Electrochemical measurements like cyclic voltammetry (CV) of synthesized GQDs were performed using a three-electrode electrochemical workstation (AEW2 Syscopel Scientific Ltd. UK) consisting of working electrode, counter electrode, and reference electrode. The CV test was carried out in the potential range of −1.5V to 1.0V at different scan rates of 5, 10, 15, 25, 50, 100mV/s. The mass load of the electrode material was 1.5mg and the electrolyte used during experiment was 0.1M aqueous solution of KCl. The specific capacitance (Cs ) of GQDs was calculated from the CV curves using the following equation [38]:
(1)
C s
=
∫
I d V
s m
Δ
V
where
∫
I d V
is the area under CV curve, m is the total mass of electroactive material on the electrode, ΔV is the voltage difference and s is the scan rate.

The energy density (Ed) can be estimated with the help of the following equation [38]:
(2)
E d =
1
2
×
C
s
×
V
2
where Cs is the specific capacitance and V is the potential vs. Ag/AgCl.

The XRD pattern of the synthesized GQDs is shown in Fig. 2 a. For comparison, the XRD pattern of raw graphite powder was also recorded; it is shown in Fig. 2b. The raw graphite powder shows the characteristic peak of graphite centered at 2θ=26.42° (JCPDS No. 41–1487) corresponding to the (002) plane with a d-spacing of 0.336nm. The diffraction peak of the GQDs is centered at 2θ=25.5° corresponding to the (002) plane with a d-spacing of 0.366nm. The appearance of a diffraction peak for the GQDs at 2θ=25.5° near to that of raw graphite powder at 2θ=26.42° indicates the restoration of destroyed network of carbon atoms after reduction. The larger d-spacing value of the GQDs indicates that synthesized GQDs still contain oxygen containing functional groups even after reduction. This could be related to the size and edge effects of GQDs. The broad nature of the diffraction peak is attributed to the nanoscale size of the GQDs with only few layers of graphene sheets [39]. Based on the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the diffraction peak, the average diameter of the crystalline size of the synthesized GQDs was estimated to be ∼2nm. The average diameter was calculated using the Debye-Scherrer formula, D =
0.9 λ
β cos θ
where D is the average diameter of the synthesized GQDs, λ is the X-ray wavelength, θ is the Bragg diffraction angle and β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM) [40].

The shape and size of the synthesized samples were investigated by TEM measurements. The TEM micrographs of the synthesized samples taken at different resolution are shown in Fig. 3 a–b. The TEM micrographs confirm the formation of mono-dispersed GQDs with almost spherical shape. However, the TEM images do not show any obvious lattice fringes of the GQDs because of the experimental limitations and sensibility of the instrument. The EDX spectrum, as shown in Fig. 3c, exhibits the elemental analysis of the synthesized GQDs. The elements present in the samples are only carbon (C) and oxygen (O). The presence of O may be due to the presence of oxygen containing functional group even after reduction of GO. The EDX spectrum indicates that the presences of C contents are higher than that of O. The higher carbon content suggests that the NH3 solution has played an important role for the restoration of the damaged sp2 carbon conjugated network. The size distribution of the synthesized GQDs was fitted by a Gaussian function; the plot is shown in Fig. 3d. The average diameter of the GQDs is determined to be ∼4nm. The small value of FWHM of the fitted Gaussian curve indicates the narrow distribution of the GQDs. The fundamental understanding of morphology evolution and growth behavior is very important in order to perform a highly controlled synthesis of the GQDs. It has been reported by Bonnemann et al. [41] that the rate of addition of reducing agent is particularly very important for the control of size and shape of the nanostructured materials. The fast rate of addition of reducing agent promotes the formation of more nanoparticles rather than growth of a particular nanoparticle while the slow addition of the reducing agent encourages the growth of a particular nanoparticle with uniform size distribution. In order to obtain highly mono-dispersed nanoparticles, nucleation must occur quickly and instantaneously. The shape of nuclei can have a great impact on the final structural morphology of the nanostructured materials. In addition, the presence of stabilizing agent prevents the uncontrolled growth and agglomeration of nanostructured materials. The stabilizing molecules are bound to the surface of the nanostructured materials stabilizing molecules towards different crystallographic faces. The surface energies of the nanostructures are thus decreased, resulting in the controlled growth and good dispersibility of the nanomaterials [42]. In the bottom up approach, the growth behavior of spherical nanostructures is explained by the classical Ostwald ripening mechanism on the basis of surface energy reduction. The final structural morphology of the nanostructured materials is determined by the reaction environment such as precursor and reducing as well as stabilizing agent concentration, the molar ratio of precursor to reducing as well as stabilizing agent, reaction time, and temperature [43].

In the present study, ammonia solution, used as a reducing and stabilizing agent, assisted the hydrothermal synthesis. It provides a powerful and convenient way for the controlled synthesis of GQDs. It should be noted that the formation of GQDs after the reduction of GO can be attributed to the direct redox reaction between the NH3 solution and GO, because there is no other reducing agent present in this method. It is expected that the π–π stacking interaction of GQDs can result in a strong binding to the NH3 molecules lying between them. Recently, Liu et al. [28] has reported that GO can be successfully reduced by the direct redox reaction between the NH2 group of benzylamine and GO [28]. It can be seen from the TEM micrographs of Fig. 3 that the GQDs synthesized by ammonia (NH3) as reducing and stabilizing agent show excellent monodispersity with controlled size and shape. Here, we would also like to note that in water, ammonia dissociates into ionic species following given reaction.
NH3 (aq)+H2O→NH4 + +OH−

OH − plays an important role in breaking down GO sheets into small fragments by reacting with defect sites of GO, which has plentiful oxygen containing functional groups. OH − also makes the surface of GO un-reactive [44]. The adjustment of volume of ammonia solution deprotonate the COOH functional group and the interaction of COO with NH4 + interlock the graphene structure and restore damaged sp2 network [45]. Besides this, the protons generated by the dissociation of water during hydrothermal reaction have a high binding affinity for oxygen containing groups, such as hydroxyl and epoxide. The protons can protonate these oxygen containing groups during binding. As a result, water molecules are formed due to the elimination of these oxygen functionalities and rGO is produced [46].

Raman spectroscopy is an effective and nondestructive technique for the characterization of carbon-based nanomaterials [47]. Structural information may easily be obtained by recording and analyzing the Raman spectra of the materials. Here, mainly the G and D bands were used for this purpose. The G band, corresponding to the E2g phonon at the Brillouin zone center, is characterizing the sp2-hybridized carbon atom network whereas the D band corresponds to the breathing modes of the sp2 atoms and is Raman-active only because of defects present in the samples [48]. Thus, the D band is used to determine the presence of defects in the carbon-based nanomaterials [47]. Fig. 4 presents the Raman spectrum of the prepared GQDs recorded in the spectral range of 1200–2800cm−1. The Raman spectrum exhibits two characteristic Raman peaks positioned at ∼1350 and ∼1590cm−1 corresponding to D and G band of GQDs, respectively [47]. It is well established that the reduction of GO would remove most of the functional groups attached to the carbon atoms and restore most of the damaged sp2 carbon conjugation. However, the presence of the D band in the Raman spectrum shows that some of the functional groups still remain in the structure of the graphene quantum dots, which creates defects in the structure. Besides the removal of functional groups, the reduction process would also introduce various defects such as vacancies and topological defects in the graphene [49]. Hence, the D band would be observed in the Raman spectra even after the efficient reduction of GO. In the present study, the stronger intensity of the G band compared to the D band indicates the high graphitization of synthesized GQDs using NH3 solution via restoration of the damaged sp2-carbon conjugated network [50,51]. The higher intensity of the G band also confirms the maximum degree of reduction of GO through deoxygenation by NH3 solution [52]. In the present case, the small D to G intensity ratio (ID/IG=0.4) not only indicates the high quality of the prepared GQDs [53], but also the usefulness of the present synthetic approach for an efficient GQDs preparation [54].

In addition, the Raman spectrum of GQDs shows a 2D band with two peaks profile, the intense 2D1 band at 2657cm−1 and a shoulder 2D2 band at 2625cm−1 due to its double resonant origin. This splitting could be due to the splitting of π electron dispersion energies because of the interaction between the neighboring graphitic planes [55]. It is important to note that the position of 2D2 band depends on the number of layers and stacking order of the graphitic sample. It is reported that the 2D band in graphitic structure exhibits a two peak profile down to 5 or 6 layers [56]. Cancado et al. [57] have reported that the 2D2 shoulder band shift upwards and merges into the 2D1 band with increasing defects in the graphitic structure. As a result, the 2D band demonstrates a single peak profile. In case of single layer graphene, the intensity of 2D band with the single peak nature must be higher than that of the G band [58].

The absorption spectrum is important for the characterization of the materials with respect to optoelectronic applications [54]. The optical properties of the GQDs are greatly influenced by the quantum confinement and edge effects caused by size reduction. The effect of size of GQDs becomes more pronounced if their size is reduced to 10nm [59]. In the present study, the optical absorption measurement for the synthesized GQDs of size ∼4nm was carried out in the wavelength range of 200–400nm. The UV–vis spectrum of the aqueous dispersion of GQDs is shown in Fig. 5 . A digital photograph of well dispersed GQDs in double-distilled water is shown in the inset of Fig. 5. The aqueous suspension of GQDs shows high stability in air at room temperature, which offers another advantage for future applications. It should also be noted that the very stable dispersion of GQDs recommends NH3 solution as a good stabilizing agent. Here, the authors believe that the polarity of the NH3 molecules is responsible for the high solubility of NH3 in water. The NH3 (polar) molecules can interact easily with solvent water molecules (polar). The interaction of NH3 and H2O molecules provide stability to the GQDs in the aqueous solution. Since, the aqueous solution of NH3 shows strong basicity, it is believed that oxygen containing functional groups and NH3 molecules are responsible for the stable aqueous dispersion of GQDs. The adjustment of the volume of NH3 deprotonate COOH functional group. Therefore, the electrostatic repulsion among the charged groups on GQDs enables the formation of well-dispersed GQDs colloids in water [45]. It can be seen from Fig. 5 that the GQDs exhibit a prominent absorption peak at ∼274nm. It corresponds to the successful reduction ability of NH3 solution to make GQDs. The origin of the characteristic absorption peak of GQDs at ∼274nm is attributed to the excitation of π-plasmons of the graphitic structure [39]. In case of the NH3 assisted reduction, the well-defined absorption peak with sharp intensity indicates that the prepared GQDs have uniform size and shape with increased concentration of π-electrons and structural ordering with the restoration of sp2-carbon atoms [60]. The present result is fully consistent with previous reports [61–63].

The photocatalytic activity of the synthesized GQDs was investigated by studying the photodegradation of aqueous solution of MB dye mixed with GQDs as photocatalyst under sunlight irradiation. It is worth to mention that using direct sunlight as excitation source was chosen in order to simulate a situation close to a realistic application where sunlight offers an inexpensive light source. To the best of our knowledge, no one has reported the photodegradation of MB dye with the help of GQDs as photocatalyst. Hence, the main objective of this work was to explore the possibility of GQDs as an excellent photocatalyst, which can be used for the photodegradation of organic pollutants under direct sunlight as it is available abundant which, in fact, reduces the entire cost of the experiment.

Fig. 8 a shows the change in the absorption spectrum of the MB dye solution irradiated by sunlight for different irradiation times. The decrease in the intensity of the characteristic absorbance band at ∼664nm is selected to monitor the degradation of MB dye. Fig. 8b compares the photodegradation rate of pure MB dye solution and dye solution with GQDs as photocatalyst. The degradation rate of MB dye without catalyst is relatively small, while the degradation rate becomes fast when GQDs are added to the dye solution. The durability of the synthesized GQDs photocatalyst was investigated by performing recycling experiment three times for the photodegradation of MB dye under sunlight. The results obtained from this experiment are shown in Fig. 8c. The photocatalytic efficiency of the synthesized GQDs displays a negligible loss of activity after three cycles. These results suggest that our GQDs have superior photocatalytic stability for sunlight driven photocatalytic degradation of MB dye molecules.

The schematic diagram of the photocatalytic mechanism with GQDs is shown in Fig. 9 . The spherical GQDs offer a large surface area to the attaching MB molecules. Therefore more MB dye molecules can interact with the catalyst, which makes the efficiency of the photodegradation rate high. When light is incident on the MB dye solution, electron (e−)-hole (h+) pairs are generated due to the induced transition from valence band (VB) electrons to the conduction band (CB) leaving a hole in the VB. These holes are allowed to react with OH ions and creates OH radicals present in the aqueous solution of MB dye while electrons may react with dissolved O2 and creates O2
− radicals into the solution. Finally, these radicals degrade MB dye molecules into small fragments [39].

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) is a suitable electrochemical measurement to characterize the capacitive behavior of the electrode materials. The rectangular CV curve, high current and symmetry in cathodic and anodic peak directions in any CV profile are the main characteristics of ideal capacitive electrode materials. Fig. 10 shows the electrochemical performance of synthesized GQDs. Fig. 10a shows the CV curves of GQDs in a potential range from −1.5V to 1.0V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at various scan rates of 5, 10, 15, 25, 50, and 100mV/s. As seen in Fig. 10a, the area of CV curve is increased with the increase of scan rate and the CV curve at different scan rates maintains the symmetrical shape, exhibiting an ideal capacitive performance of the synthesized GQDs. The symmetrical shape of the CV curves over a broad range of scan rates is an extremely important feature for its applications in making supercapacitors [31]. In addition, the observable increase of current with the increase of scan rates in the CV curve of Fig. 10a indicates a good rate capability of GQDs. It can also be seen that the cathodic peak position shifts positively and the anodic peak position shifts negatively with increasing scan rates, which is normally due to the resistance of the electrode. As the scan rate increases, the energy loss rises and the charge stored on the electrode surface falls. This causes the capacitance efficiency of electrode materials to decrease. The large surface area of GQDs creates more contact interface between the GQDs and the electrolyte for electrochemical energy conversion [73].

Fig. 10b shows the cyclic performance of GQDs electrodes at the scan rate of 10mV/s. All the CV curves exhibit a quasi-rectangular shape and are symmetrical in nature, which indicates the ideal supercapacitor characteristics of GQDs. The quasi-rectangular shape also shows superior charge propagation within the electrodes. Here, it is interesting to note that the CV curves of GQDs (Fig. 10b) have no obvious redox peaks, which further suggests that GQDs possesses an electrical double layer capacitor behavior. It is also reported that, in general, double layer capacitors show enhanced electrochemical stability compared to faradaic capacitors. The graphene layers in GQDs interact with each other and create open pore systems, through which ions simply enter into the surfaces between the graphene layers to form an electric double layer [74]. The cycle stability of the GQDs electrode is examined by repeating the CV measurements at a scan rate of 10mV/s for 5 cycles, which demonstrate a good cyclic performance and reversibility of the GQDs. Cyclic stability of electrode materials is an important parameter for practical applications. The CV profiles found in the present case are very similar to those previously reported for graphene derivatives [74]. From Fig. 10b, it can be seen that GQDs electrodes show a minor decrease in the value of peak position current in the subsequent cycles, which refers to little current loss. This validates to a good cycling stability of the synthesized GQDs.

The specific capacitance (Cs in F/g) of the GQDs electrodes is calculated using Eq. (1). The value of Cs decreases with increasing scan rate, as shown in Fig. 10c. It is clear from Fig. 10c that the value of Cs is as high as 481F/g at a slow scan rate of 5mV/s and it is as low as 41F/g at a fast scan rate of 100mV/s. The value of Cs decreases from 481 to 41F/g as the scan rate is increased from 5 to 100mV/s. The decrease in the value of Cs is attributed to diffusion limits of electrolyte ions. It also suggests that only the outer surface of the GQDs electrodes is active for charge storage, while the inner surface cannot maintain the redox transitions. Therefore, the value of Cs at the slowest scan rate is the actual value of the GQDs electrodes. The dependence of the energy density of the GQDs electrode on the scan rate is shown in Fig. 10d. The outstanding energy density of GQDs at different scan-rates may originate from the high electrical conductivity and high surface area of the GQDs [75]. Hence, the excellent electrochemical performance of the GQDs indicates that GQDs may be a capable electrode material for high performance energy storage supercapacitors.

A facile and low-cost synthesis approach has been employed to prepare monodispersed spherical GQDs in aqueous solution using ammonia solution both as reducing and stabilizing agent. The results demonstrate that the present approach is effective to produce a narrow size distribution of GQDs with well-controlled morphology. The structural and optical studies show that the present method exerts a strong influence on the formation, microstructure, and optical properties of GQDs. Interestingly, the GQDs revealed tunable PL emission (490–550nm) by simply varying the excitation wavelength (350–450) without changing the experimental conditions. The tunable emission could be due to the transition of σ orbital electrons to a high energy state and subsequently return to a lower energy state of the π orbital. In addition to the tunable emission, the synthesized GQDs also show excellent photocatalytic and electrochemical activities, which makes them promising candidates for applications as photocatalytic and electrode materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: SK is thankful to University Grants Commission, India for providing financial support through D. S. Kothari Postdoctoral Fellowship Scheme (No.F.4-2/2006(BSR)/PH/15-16/0067). AKO are thankful to the Department of Science and Technology (DST), New Delhi for providing the financial support through research grant SR/FTP/PS-038/2011. AKO is thankful to the AvH foundation, Germany for providing the financial support to visit Jacobs University, Bremen, Germany.
